concept_name: Maximum Height — How High the Projectile Rises
source_book: DC Pandey 7.6 — projectile maximum height; split from projectile_motion

real_world_anchor.primary: A cricket six at Wankhede stadium. Launched at 30 m/s, angle 45 degrees. Maximum height reached = (v * sin(theta))^2 / (2g) = (30 * 0.707)^2 / 20 ≈ 22.5 metres. That is higher than the stadium's floodlight towers — why commentators often say 'it's going into the stands' the moment the ball leaves the bat. Every six-hitting batsman has an internal model of this number.
real_world_anchor.secondary: An Indian Army mortar on an artillery range in Pokhran. Shell launched at 100 m/s, angle 60 degrees. Max height = (100 * sin(60))^2 / 20 ≈ 375 metres — three times the height of the Qutub Minar. That altitude is why artillery rounds can clear buildings and land behind enemy lines.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A volleyball player spiking at the Indian national tournament. Jumped takeoff adds ~2 m/s vertical to a launch at 18 m/s at 70 degrees. Max height = (18 * sin(70))^2 / 20 ≈ 14.3 m above the ball's release point — useful for calculating court hang time.

formulas.derivation_path: At peak, vertical velocity = 0.  Apply v_y^2 = v_y0^2 - 2gH with v_y = 0 to solve.
formulas.time_to_peak: t_peak = v0 * sin(theta) / g  (half of total flight time)
formulas.relation_to_launch_angle: H depends on sin^2(theta); peak at theta = 90 deg (straight up).
formulas.unequal_heights: Not applicable if launch and landing are at different heights; use energy conservation or kinematics with h0.

constraints: Only vertical component contributes to height (horizontal component does not).
H = 0 at theta = 0 (horizontal shot never rises).
H is maximum at theta = 90 deg = v0^2 / (2g).

STATE_1.title: Peak is Where Vertical Velocity Equals Zero

scene_composition.text:
At the highest point of any projectile's flight:

     vertical velocity  v_y  =  0

  Not the TOTAL speed -- just the vertical component.
  The ball is still moving HORIZONTALLY at v0 * cos(theta),
  but its UP-ness has momentarily paused.

                      ~peak~
                     /      \
                    /        \
                   /          \
                  /            \
                 /              \
                /                \
              launch            landing

  The peak is when gravity has fully used up
  the initial upward velocity.

tts_sentences.text_en: At the peak of any projectile's flight, the VERTICAL velocity is zero — but only the vertical component.
The ball is still moving horizontally at v-nought cosine theta; its up-ness has briefly paused.
Why does this happen? Gravity has been pulling downward the entire time, chipping away at the initial upward velocity.
When all the upward speed is used up, the ball stops rising — that is the peak of the trajectory.

scene_composition.text:
Derivation using v_y^2 = v_y0^2 - 2 g H:

  At the peak,  v_y = 0,  so:
     0^2  =  (v0 * sin(theta))^2  -  2 * g * H

  Solve for H:
     H  =  (v0 * sin(theta))^2  /  (2 * g)

  Expanded:
     H  =  v0^2  *  sin^2(theta)  /  (2 * g)

  Numbers for a Wankhede six:
     v0 = 30,  theta = 45 deg,  g = 10
     v0 * sin(theta)  =  30 * 0.707  =  21.2 m/s
     H  =  21.2^2 / 20  =  450 / 20  =  22.5 m

  Ball rises 22.5 m above bat height -- higher than floodlights.

tts_sentences.text_en: Use the kinematics equation v-y squared equals v-y-zero squared minus 2 g H.
At the peak v-y is zero. Plug in and solve for H.
H equals v-nought sine theta squared, all divided by 2 g.
For a Wankhede six at 30 metres per second, 45 degrees — H is 22.5 metres. Higher than the stadium's floodlights, which is exactly why the crowd roars before the ball even lands.

scene_composition.text:
H = v0^2 * sin^2(theta) / (2g)

  Table for v0 = 20 m/s, g = 10:

   theta = 15 deg  ->  H  =  400 * sin^2(15)/20  =   1.34 m
   theta = 30 deg  ->  H  =  400 * 0.25      /20  =   5.00 m
   theta = 45 deg  ->  H  =  400 * 0.5       /20  =  10.00 m
   theta = 60 deg  ->  H  =  400 * 0.75      /20  =  15.00 m
   theta = 75 deg  ->  H  =  400 * sin^2(75)/20  =  18.66 m
   theta = 90 deg  ->  H  =  400 * 1.0       /20  =  20.00 m  <- max

  Unlike range (which peaks at 45 deg),
  height keeps INCREASING as angle approaches 90 deg.

  Hence straight-up throw (theta=90) gives max height
  but zero horizontal progress.

tts_sentences.text_en: Max height depends on sine-SQUARED theta — not sine theta.
At 30 degrees you get 5 metres. At 45 — 10. At 60 — 15. At 75 — 18.66. At 90 — a full 20 metres.
Unlike range which peaks at 45 degrees, height keeps climbing all the way to 90 degrees.
A straight-up throw gives the maximum possible height but zero horizontal distance. An angle tradeoff every projectile problem rests on.

epic_c_branches.misconception_summary: Student uses the full launch speed v0 instead of its vertical component in the max-height formula.
confusion_patterns: max height use v0 not v0 sin theta
why sin theta in max height

scene_composition.text:
Student's wrong shortcut:
  "Projectile with speed v0 -- use energy:
   (1/2) v0^2 = g * H  ->  H = v0^2 / (2g)."

  That only works for a PURELY VERTICAL throw.

  For a projectile launched at angle theta:
    Kinetic energy has both horizontal AND vertical parts.
    Only the VERTICAL part is spent rising.
    Horizontal part keeps moving sideways all along.

  So H = v0^2 sin^2(theta) / (2g), not v0^2 / (2g).

  Using v0 instead of v0 sin(theta) OVERSTATES H.

tts_sentences.text_en: Wrong shortcut — student writes H equals v-nought squared over 2 g using the full speed.
That only works if the throw is PURELY vertical. Otherwise, you have just assumed the entire kinetic energy goes into rising.
At angle theta, only the vertical part, v-nought sine theta, rises. The horizontal part keeps moving sideways, and its energy stays kinetic — it never converts to height.

scene_composition.text:
Energy conservation at the peak:

  At launch:
     KE_total = (1/2) * m * v0^2

  At peak:
     vertical velocity = 0
     horizontal velocity = v0 * cos(theta)  (unchanged)
     KE_remaining = (1/2) * m * (v0 * cos(theta))^2

  Energy lost to raise ball to height H:
     delta_KE = KE_total - KE_remaining
             = (1/2) * m * [ v0^2 - v0^2 * cos^2(theta) ]
             = (1/2) * m * v0^2 * [ 1 - cos^2(theta) ]
             = (1/2) * m * v0^2 * sin^2(theta)

  Set equal to m * g * H:
     H = v0^2 * sin^2(theta) / (2g)

  Same answer -- derived from energy.  Horizontal KE never disappears.

tts_sentences.text_en: Let us cure this mistake with energy conservation.
At launch, the projectile has kinetic energy half m v-nought squared.
At the peak, only the horizontal velocity remains — the projectile still has kinetic energy half m v-nought cosine theta squared.
The difference is what went into raising the projectile by height H. That difference is half m v-nought squared sine squared theta.
Set equal to m g H, cancel m, rearrange — you get H equals v-nought squared sine squared theta over 2 g. Same as before.

scene_composition.text:
Check the formula with limits:

   theta = 0 deg  (horizontal shot):
      sin(0) = 0  -> H = 0  (correct, never rises)

   theta = 90 deg  (straight up):
      sin(90) = 1  -> H = v0^2 / (2g)  (correct, max)

   theta = 45 deg:
      sin^2(45) = 0.5  -> H = v0^2 / (4g)
      -- exactly half of the vertical-throw maximum

  Formula passes every edge case.
  If your answer differs, you used the wrong formula.

tts_sentences.text_en: Limit checks. At zero degrees — horizontal shot — sine is zero, so H is zero. Correct, ball never rises.
At 90 degrees — straight up — sine is one, so H is v-nought squared over 2g. Correct, this is the maximum.
At 45 degrees — sine-squared is half, so H is v-nought squared over 4g — exactly half the vertical maximum.
Formula passes every edge case. If your answer disagrees, you used the wrong formula.
